reason: The identity R^2=(S^2)^(-1) is asserted as if it follows from 'R=f(S^2) is a norm-limit of polynomials in S^2', but that property only yields that R commutes with S and S^2; it is logically insufficient for the inverse-square identity. Proving R^2=(S^2)^(-1) for the concretely-defined series R=sum_k C(-1/2,k)U^k (node 1.2, U=S^2-I) requires: (i) the Cauchy-product theorem for absolutely convergent series in the Banach algebra End(B(H)_sa), giving R^2=sum_m (sum_{k+j=m} C(-1/2,k)C(-1/2,j)) U^m; (ii) the binomial-coefficient convolution identity sum_{k+j=m} C(-1/2,k)C(-1/2,j)=(-1)^m; and (iii) the Neumann series sum_m (-1)^m U^m=(I+U)^(-1)=(S^2)^(-1). None of (i),(ii),(iii) is cited or provenanced. The entire node hinges on this: sgn(S)^2=S^2 R^2=I uses exactly R^2=(S^2)^(-1), and P^2=P reduces to sgn(S)^2=I.
